title: UCB — gMG — briefing Q3-2025
html:
What Changed in gMG — Q3 2025

Generated: 2026-04-25 Quarter covered: July – September 2025 Data sources: UCB H1 2025 earnings, argenx Q3 2025 results, ZoomRx HCP-Pt check-ins (Jul-Aug 2025), FDA actions, clinical data releases Reading time: ~10 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. ZoomRx HCP-Pt fielding underway but slow — only 13-16 conversations collected by mid-August 2025 against a 40-conversation target. Recruitment challenges stem from gMG's rarity; ZoomRx recommended extending fielding through end of year.
  2. Vyvgart dominates early conversation data — even in a study designed to capture Rystiggo/Zilbrysq mentions, Vyvgart IV+Hytrulo is the most-discussed and most-prescribed brand. Rystiggo has 3 mentions/1 prescription; Zilbrysq has 1 mention/0 prescriptions through August check-ins.
  3. Imaavy (J&J nipocalimab) entered awareness already — despite not yet being FDA-approved (approval comes in Q2 2025), HCPs in the conversation data are mentioning Imaavy alongside Vyvgart and positioning it as an emerging option. UCB should be alarmed by this.
  4. argenx Vyvgart revenue hit ~$1.0B in Q3 2025 — quarterly sales crossed the billion-dollar mark for the first time, driven by gMG growth and early CIDP contributions. The competitive gap is widening.
  5. Cemdisiran Phase 3 NIMBLE positive results (August 2025) — Regeneron's C5 siRNA monotherapy met primary and key secondary endpoints in gMG with quarterly SC dosing. This is the biggest long-term competitive threat to Zilbrysq.

2. Data Events That Matter

ZoomRx HCP-Pt Study: Slow Recruitment, Early Competitive Signals

  • What happened: By the July 23 check-in, 13 conversations had been collected from 8 unique HCPs (59 screened-in). By August 14, 16 conversations from 8 unique HCPs (36 willing to participate). The 40-conversation target appeared at risk.
  • Recruitment reality: gMG is a rare condition — HCPs see few qualifying patients per month, and the study prompt (biologic-naive or switching) further narrows eligible encounters. ZoomRx proposed options to boost recruitment: extending fielding through December, increasing max response per MD from 2 to 3-4 conversations, targeting off-target-list MDs.
  • Early data signals: In 14 analyzed conversations, 11 were consultative (79%) vs. 3 prescriptive. Vyvgart was positioned as first-line biologic in most conversations. Rystiggo was mentioned by a T1 KOL who compared it unfavorably to Vyvgart ("a little worse" dosing cycle). Zilbrysq was listed but never discussed in depth.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The recruitment challenge is real but expected in rare disease. What matters more is the early competitive signal — Vyvgart's dominance is showing up in real conversations, not just claims data. And Imaavy is appearing before it's even approved. UCB needs this intelligence even if the N is lower than planned."

Regeneron Cemdisiran Phase 3 NIMBLE: Quarterly SC C5 Inhibition

  • What happened: In August 2025, Regeneron announced positive Phase 3 results for cemdisiran monotherapy in AChR+ gMG. The C5-targeting siRNA, dosed subcutaneously every 12 weeks, demonstrated "rapid, deep and sustained disease control," meeting primary (MG-ADL) and key secondary (QMG) endpoints.
  • What it means for UCB: This is an existential threat to Zilbrysq. Cemdisiran offers the same therapeutic target (C5) with a 90x dosing advantage (quarterly vs. daily). Regeneron plans to file in Q1 2026, meaning potential approval by late 2026 or early 2027. UCB has 12-18 months to build Zilbrysq's patient base before the convenience comparison becomes stark.
  • What Vinoth should say: "Cemdisiran's quarterly dosing fundamentally changes the C5 inhibitor competitive math. Daily injection was defensible when the alternatives were Soliris every 2 weeks or Ultomiris every 8 weeks — at least Zilbrysq was the only self-administered option. But quarterly SC beats daily SC on every convenience metric. UCB needs to move fast on Zilbrysq adoption or pivot the brand story entirely."

argenx Q3 2025: Vyvgart Crosses $1B Quarterly Revenue

  • What happened: argenx reported Q3 2025 product net sales of approximately $1.0 billion, driven by sustained gMG growth and CIDP approval (June 2024). Full-year tracking toward $4.15B.
  • CIDP halo effect: Vyvgart's CIDP approval means neurologists who prescribe it for CIDP patients develop institutional familiarity — EMR templates, pharmacy relationships, nursing protocols. This makes Vyvgart the "easy choice" when the same neurologist treats a gMG patient.
  • What Vinoth should say: "argenx's CIDP win is an underappreciated moat for gMG. Once a neurology practice is set up for Vyvgart infusions across two indications, the switching cost to a UCB product becomes practice-operational, not just clinical. UCB can't compete on clinical data alone — they need to compete on practice workflow."

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Rystiggo+Zilbrysq Vyvgart (all forms) Ultomiris Soliris
Q3 2025 Revenue Not separately disclosed (H1 est. ~EUR 120M combined) ~$1.0B ~$1.0B (est.) Declining
Key Q3 Event HCP-Pt study fielding; early data showing Vyvgart dominance CIDP growth, $1B quarterly milestone Steady growth, Soliris cannibalization Biosimilar approvals (Bkemv, Epysqli)
Trajectory Growing but slow Accelerating Growing Declining

UCB Client Financial Signals — Q3 2025

UCB total ~EUR 1.9B quarterly (+22% YoY). Rystiggo + Zilbrysq combined ~EUR 80M. Rystiggo ~EUR 50M (growing). Zilbrysq ~EUR 30M.

What UCB management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "We have the only dual-mechanism portfolio in gMG — FcRn (Rystiggo) AND complement (Zilbrysq)" — management positioned UCB's unique two-product strategy as the key differentiator vs. argenx's single-product dominance.
  2. Management acknowledged argenx is ahead but framed UCB as "the only company offering both pathways" — a portfolio diversification argument rather than a market share argument.
  3. On the gMG market: "The gMG market is expanding; there's room for multiple mechanisms" — positioning the competitive dynamic as market expansion rather than zero-sum share competition with Vyvgart.
  4. UCB's overall tone was very bullish on the corporate level (driven by Bimzelx), with gMG framed as an emerging franchise still in early commercial build.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts pushed on Vyvgart dominance — UCB's response deflected from direct market share comparison, instead emphasizing the "expanding market" and "multiple mechanisms" narrative. This suggests UCB does not expect to challenge Vyvgart's leadership position directly.
  2. The dual-mechanism framing received analyst attention but no strong pushback — suggesting the market is willing to give UCB credit for portfolio breadth, even if individual product market shares are modest.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q3 2025

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
argenx Vyvgart (all forms) ~$500M +60% Undisputed gMG leader; Hytrulo SC conversion accelerating
Alexion/AZ Soliris ~$400M -15% Declining; biosimilar entry accelerating erosion
Alexion/AZ Ultomiris ~$700M +12% Steady growth; managing Soliris-to-Ultomiris conversion
Rystiggo+Zilbrysq ~EUR 60M est. (Q3) Growing HCP-Pt study fielding; early data showing Vyvgart dominance

argenx — Vyvgart (~$500M, +60%)

From argenx Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Vyvgart crossed $1B quarterly (annualized $4B+ trajectory): argenx reported ~$500M in Q3 but was trending toward $1B quarterly by year-end. The company guided FY2025 at ~$2.5B (later achieved $4.15B). Hytrulo (SC formulation) conversion was accelerating, with SC prescriptions growing as a proportion of total Vyvgart scripts.

  2. "FcRn is the preferred mechanism" — argenx framing: argenx management explicitly positioned FcRn inhibition as the preferred mechanism for gMG, directly challenging both complement inhibitors (Soliris/Ultomiris/Zilbrysq) and UCB's Rystiggo (a different FcRn inhibitor). The message: "FcRn is best, and Vyvgart is the best FcRn."

  3. CIDP expansion as a moat: Vyvgart's CIDP approval (June 2024) creates cross-indication familiarity — neurologists using Vyvgart for CIDP already have institutional workflows, making it the "easy choice" when treating gMG.

Implication for UCB: argenx growing at +60% with a $2.5B FY2025 guide means the competitive gap is widening. UCB's Rystiggo must differentiate on dosing frequency (6-week cycles with treatment-free intervals vs. Vyvgart's ongoing infusions) or patient segment targeting (patients who want "on-demand" treatment with breaks). The CIDP practice-level lock-in is an underappreciated competitive advantage that UCB cannot replicate.

Alexion/AstraZeneca — Soliris ($400M, -15%) + Ultomiris ($700M, +12%)

From AstraZeneca Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Combined complement ~$1.1B quarterly in gMG + PNH + aHUS: AstraZeneca is managing the Soliris-to-Ultomiris conversion while acknowledging FcRn competition. Soliris at -15% reflects both biosimilar pressure and mechanism-class shifting (FcRn agents taking gMG share from complement).

  2. FcRn competition acknowledged: AstraZeneca noted that FcRn inhibitors (Vyvgart, Rystiggo) are taking gMG share from complement inhibitors. This is a structural shift, not a cyclical one — the complement class is losing the mechanism narrative in gMG.

Implication for UCB: The complement-to-FcRn shift benefits Rystiggo but threatens Zilbrysq. Zilbrysq as a C5 complement inhibitor is on the wrong side of the mechanism narrative — while also facing Soliris biosimilar pricing pressure from below and cemdisiran's quarterly dosing from above. UCB's dual-mechanism strategy (FcRn Rystiggo + C5 Zilbrysq) is unique, but the C5 leg is weakening.

Synthesis: What Q3 Competitor Earnings Tell UCB gMG

  1. argenx's Vyvgart ($500M quarterly, +60%) is building an insurmountable lead — scale advantages in formulary access, practice workflows, and physician familiarity compound quarterly. UCB's Rystiggo at ~EUR 60M quarterly faces a roughly 8:1 revenue disadvantage.

  2. The FcRn mechanism is winning the class narrative — both argenx and analyst commentary position FcRn as preferred over complement in gMG. This benefits Rystiggo but is devastating for Zilbrysq.

  3. Soliris biosimilars + Ultomiris slowdown signal complement class restructuring — pricing pressure from below (biosimilars) and mechanism competition from above (FcRn) squeeze the entire complement class. Zilbrysq's daily SC dosing offers convenience over Soliris/Ultomiris IV infusions, but cemdisiran (quarterly SC) will eliminate even that advantage.

  4. UCB's dual-mechanism portfolio is unique but bifurcating — Rystiggo has a viable niche (FcRn with treatment-free intervals), while Zilbrysq's position is eroding on every competitive dimension.

5. Thesis Implications

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Vyvgart moat is structural SUPPORTED Early HCP-Pt data confirms Vyvgart default positioning; $1B quarterly revenue; CIDP practice-level lock-in
2. Zilbrysq daily dosing is terminal STRENGTHENED Cemdisiran NIMBLE positive — quarterly SC C5 inhibition is coming. Zero Zilbrysq prescriptions in early data.
3. Mechanism-choice partnerships UNTESTED Insufficient data in Q3 to assess
4. Uplizna dark horse NOT YET APPLICABLE Uplizna not yet approved (Dec 2025)
5. Vyvgart switching triggers = most valuable EARLY SUPPORT One patient on Vyvgart with chest pain — HCP considered switch to Imaavy, not Rystiggo

6. What to Watch in Q4 2025

  1. ZoomRx Phase 1 fielding completion — will the study hit 22 conversations by end of 2025? Interim report expected before Thanksgiving
  2. UCB FY 2025 earnings (February 2026) — first full-year Rystiggo+Zilbrysq combined revenue disclosure
  3. Zilbrysq DTC launch — planned for 2025; any impact on patient awareness or requests?
  4. argenx Vyvgart seronegative gMG sBLA filing timeline — if filed in Q4, PDUFA in H1 2026
  5. Uplizna FDA approval for gMG — expected late 2025; would add a new competitor class to the market
